title: Cacio e pepe
subtitle: Roman pasta with pecorino and black pepper
excerpt: Spaghetti or tonnarelli tossed with finely grated pecorino Romano, parmesan, and a large amount of coarsely cracked black pepper, emulsified with starchy pasta water into a sauce that is thick, creamy, and pungent. Three ingredients, one technique, no shortcuts.
sourceNotes: Cacio e pepe (cheese and pepper) is one of the three great Roman pasta dishes alongside carbonara and amatriciana. It is documented from the eighteenth century as a staple of Roman shepherds, a dish of dried pasta, aged sheep's cheese, and black pepper, requiring no refrigeration. The technique of emulsifying cheese and starchy water into a cream is a specific Roman skill. The simplicity is deceptive.

content.text: The single technique that determines whether cacio e pepe works or turns into a lump of scrambled cheese is temperature control. The cheese must go in off the heat or with the pan well away from any direct flame. It then needs to be worked (tossed or stirred) with starchy pasta water until it emulsifies into a cream. Hot pan plus cheese equals clumps. Room-temperature pan plus cheese plus starchy water plus movement equals sauce.

The pasta water must be starchy, use a small amount of water for the pasta quantity, so the water becomes concentrated. Grate the cheese finely: a coarse grate does not emulsify smoothly. Toast the black pepper in a dry pan before cracking it; the toasting opens up the volatile aromatics and gives the dish its heat without bitterness.

items.name: Black pepper
items.prepNote: whole, toasted in a dry pan, then coarsely cracked

content.text: Toast the pepper and cook the pasta

Toast the {{black-pepper}} corns in a dry large frying pan over medium heat for 2 minutes until fragrant. Crack them coarsely with a pestle or the base of a heavy pan, aiming for rough, uneven pieces rather than fine powder. Set the pan aside. Cook the {{pasta-dried}} in well-salted boiling water, use a smaller pot than usual so the water becomes starchy. Reserve 200 ml of pasta water before draining. Drain, leaving the pasta slightly wet.

Return the frying pan with the cracked pepper to medium-low heat. Add 60 ml of the pasta water and let it come to a simmer, swirling to create a pepper-infused liquid. Add the drained pasta and toss to coat. Remove the pan from the heat entirely. Add the {{pecorino-romano}} and {{parmesan}} in a continuous rain over the pasta, tossing rapidly with tongs or two forks while adding splashes of warm pasta water a tablespoon at a time. The cheese should melt into a cohesive cream in 1 to 2 minutes of tossing. Taste and add more pepper if needed. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Remove the pan completely from any heat source before the cheese goes in; the pasta's residual heat is enough if you toss fast
items.cause: Pan was too hot when the cheese was added
items.symptom: Cheese has clumped into rubbery lumps

items.fix: Add warm pasta water a tablespoon at a time, tossing between each addition, until the sauce reaches a flowing consistency
items.symptom: Sauce is too thick and dense

items.fix: Use less water when boiling the pasta; keep the pasta wet when draining; the surface moisture is what begins the emulsification
items.cause: Pasta water not starchy enough or pasta too dry when cheese was added
items.symptom: No sauce at all, just dry pasta with cheese flakes

items.fix: Toasting the whole peppercorns first rounds out the flavour and reduces bitterness; 2 tsp is correct for 2 servings, adjust to taste
items.cause: Black pepper not toasted, or too much pepper used
items.symptom: Pepper dominates everything and is bitter

content.text: Cacio e pepe belongs to Rome, like carbonara. Both are Roman pasta dishes that the rest of Italy makes badly or differently. Romans guard them fiercely. Cacio e pepe is older than carbonara by at least two hundred years. It was here before guanciale came into Roman pasta cooking, and it is much simpler. Three things, one skill. The skill is learned, not natural. The first few tries often fail. The cheese clumps, or the sauce goes watery, before you find the right balance.
